steps.title: Convert radical to exponent form and substitute
steps.content: Rewrite the left side using exponent rules: $\sqrt[5]{p^3} = p^{\frac{3}{5}}$, so the equation becomes $p^{\frac{3}{5}} = t^{\frac{8}{7}}$. Now substitute the given expression $t = p^{3n-1}$: $$p^{\frac{3}{5}} = (p^{3n-1})^{\frac{8}{7}}$$

steps.title: Apply power rule and equate exponents
steps.content: Use the power rule $(a^m)^n = a^{mn}$ on the right side: $$p^{\frac{3}{5}} = p^{(3n-1) \cdot \frac{8}{7}} = p^{\frac{8(3n-1)}{7}}$$ Since $p \gt  1$, the bases are equal, so the exponents must be equal: $$\frac{3}{5} = \frac{8(3n-1)}{7}$$

steps.title: Solve for n
steps.content:
Cross-multiply to clear the fractions: $$3 \cdot 7 = 5 \cdot 8(3n-1)$$ $$21 = 40(3n-1)$$ Expand the right side: $$21 = 120n - 40$$ Add 40 to both sides: $$61 = 120n$$ Therefore: $$n = \frac{61}{120}$$
The answer is 61/120.
